speaker: interviewee
text: And the father was making wine the Russians liked very much, and it was very good for us. Then one day, on a Sunday, he gets up very early. I am helping him out. They put in-- we had a big kettle that cooked honey. Maybe like 200 pound of honey, 100 pound of sugar. We put it in on Sunday. I was a student, but on Sunday I helped him out putting in that stuff. It was very early in the morning. We put in the honey. We put in the sugar. And I'm mixing-- mixing it up, on a Sunday. We hear someone's knocking on the door. He says, don't open up. I want to put in everything there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He put in everything there, then opened the door. Who was it? A woman. Her husband worked for the Communists. He's a chauffeur, a driver. She says, you know what? It's a war now. The Germans came in on us. We already put in the sugar and the honey, like 300 pounds. 10 days later, the Germans came in. We had-- they came in. They say-- they-- to [INAUDIBLE]. They say they want a drink. Can you bring some in the house to get the wine, drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each time the Germans come in-- four or five Germans-- to drink, my father has to drink first because they are afraid that it's-- it poison. All day Saturday-- this was Saturday-- my father had a stomach like that. He couldn't take anymore. It was the evening. We opened windows. And we had maybe 500 liters wine. Me and father took [? out ?] the wine and he threw that in the garden. So it was empty. The next day, we didn't have no wine. And two days later came the Germans, and they took everything made of copper. The kettle and pipes. They took it out and just took it away from us-- from-- from us. Because they were making ammunition from the-- from the copper. And this was the end of the factory, of the wine factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '41. Like I tell you, it was the-- the [INAUDIBLE] when they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened after you lost the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, we were-- we-- I-- they were kind of foolish, we hide clothing in a grave and covered it up. We didn't have no food. So I know that to have food. You have to have some clothing. Like, this was hide and covered on the ground, but didn't dig it back up. He was very hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one-- we had a-- a wagon, a horse wagon. We found one. We sold-- sold it to somebody, and he gave us maybe 100 kilogram flour. So we could leave a couple months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we cooked from the flour. We made bread. Germans-- young Germans came in. They took away everything from-- from-- from the thing. But in our yard was the other Germans, older ones, [? sanitary. ?] They say that-- that you're carrying their bread. You know. They say, give them back their bread. And then they took-- give us back the bread. And they-- they run away. And that Germans-- the young ones-- shows me their fists and said they're gonna kill me. But the older ones protect us. They were [? senators. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there. It was maybe a couple months. We had to go to work, so we went to the sugar factory. Start work in the sugar factory, me and Father. This was the end of our wine factory. And we had to work for the sugar factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we worked in the sugar factory, I always had the mind to learn something. And in this sugar factory, they took a couple Jewish guy making leather. Making belts for the machinery. At lunch time, I come in and warming up. In the meantime, I see what they're doing. This helped me to save my life. When I was hiding, I came in to a farmer and said, I can do sugar leather. And each farmer had a few piece of-- hides, and I'm going to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they give me the-- their food, and I was worked by that farmer a couple of hides. And then by that farmer a couple of hides. And each [? layer, ?] I went to a different farmer-- this was later on in the woods. In the-- in March, April in '40-- '43 already. That was the beginning-- what-- that's what saved me. I was worked by the farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then later on, like I told you, they want to have whiskey. So you have flour, but you need yeast. They don't have yeast. I'm thinking by myself, if I could get a little bit of yeast, I could produce more yeast. One farmer girl, 50 miles from us, she-- I hear she has 15 gram of yeast. And she wants so much money. I had a few pennies. So I went to her, I went 15 miles. And I-- in the morning, it took-- took me half a day. I came in-- maybe later.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the minute I came in, she says, what you're doing here? The-- I [? have ?] partisans was against the Jews. They kill-- if they see you, they're gonna kill you. And she hides me in the barn. When they gonna go away, I'm going to deal with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go in the barn. It's 4 o'clock in the afternoon. I'm staying there. The partisans are still there until the evening. I'm so hungry, all day I didn't eat anything. In the evening, I see some pickles. Some-- I grab the pickles, I didn't think much, and I eat four or five pickles. Then I was so tired, I went in a corner over there in the barn, and I fall asleep. Then I wake up with pain in my stomach. And I cannot cry because they are staying outside the-- the barn, and they can listen to me. I open my shirt, I put my shirt in my-- and eating at my shirt from the pain. Two, three hours-- I don't know how long [? in ?] the pain. I eat up all the shirt with my teeth. Then I threw up. After I threw up, I felt better. I went in another corner and fell asleep. I was asleep all day-- all night. And next morning, she came and she said, the partisans went away. She gave me the yeast. I paid her the money. And I came-- and I went back up the 50 miles. And from that little yeast, I made more yeast in bottles. I was going around to the farmers saying that I have yeast. You can make moonshine. Nobody wants to believe me. Nobody wants to take from me. One guy [INAUDIBLE] is the first one. He says, I'm gonna take it. If this doesn't work, you better watch out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took from me the two liter. And he made that thing for-- for moonshine, and it came out moonshine. And he tells everybody that this guy, Jewish guy, has yeast. And they start giving to me flour for the yeast. And I was-- then the Jewish-- in the woods, they have another Jewish-- they say, oh, we have good-- you have food. Why don't you get somebody else to help you? So what am I going to take? Take a girl? I'm not an angel. [INAUDIBLE] or something. I'm not taking. I'm taking-- I took a boy with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took him the four miles. He had to walk with us. I didn't realize that he was very sick. He couldn't make the four miles. He was at the [INAUDIBLE]. I took him-- come in-- on the barn. I laid him down in the barn and stayed there a couple days. I give him my clothing. I threw his clothing away because I know that if he has something that-- then I can get it. I burned his clothing and gave him my clothing. And he stayed with me all the time. His name was Bill Kuperschmidt from Brest-- from Brest-Litovsk. And we make yeast, we make the-- the leather goods, and that's the way we supporting ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of time the partisans want-- wants to take us, but we have to get guns. 'Cause other guns they didn't-- [INAUDIBLE]. And you have to go from one farmer to the other, and you're in danger. They see you Jewish, they don't-- won't take a penny [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with Bill Kuperschmidt. I stayed there all winter, till '43 in January, the year the Russians came in. They didn't-- wasn't in the woods, they came in the cities. So in January-- end of January, beginning of February, I have some flour. Me and a few other guys get horse and wagon. Getting a-- a guy in his-- wherever we are belonging. We have to walk 40 miles to our town. We're going back to our town. So they get us [INAUDIBLE]. I was and young guy, I never sit down. There's a few other guys there who was sitting down, they work a couple days. And they came into our town. Back, the Russian was there in February month. And I came in, where are we gonna sleep? So a Jewish guy says to me, [INAUDIBLE] all the-- the houses are taken by Poles, because the Ukrainian killed the Poles. So the Poles, the farmers, run away from the-- from the farms, and they're sitting in the city. You going into that house, it's a [INAUDIBLE] home. Tell that your house, that's your uncle's house, and she will let you in. That's OK. I came in there, and I says, I-- this is my uncle's house, and she let us in-- me and Bill, the-- the house. So I shared to sleep. What do you do? He didn't have anywhere to sleep. So I met another guy. He gave me a-- a bed. So I brought the bed in and me and Bill stayed in the bed. All the guy says, you have to register. They're going in the army. I says, I'm not going in the army. I'm not registering. The guys that registered, they calling them into the army. It was on Friday, they have to go in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thursday night, in the evening, I'm going in market buy a piece of bread. They caught me over there-- the police. And they took me to the-- to the place. I'm going in there and maybe 200 people there already in [? the hall. ?] And I'm sitting at that [INAUDIBLE] too, there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the evening comes in, a friend of mine, he was a secretary in the-- for-- worked for the Russians. He says, why are you coming in here? Tomorrow they're gonna send you on-- on the front. He gives me his coat, his-- his hat, and his briefcase. He says, go through and say you work in here. It was dark anyway, because they were afraid for light for the airplanes. And say you work here. That's what he did. He gave me his coat. He gave me his briefcase. I passed by, the guards ask me who I am. I says, I'm working here. He lets me-- let me out. I'm coming back to my-- to the city there. And I'm preparing a package for Bill. When I'm preparing the package 10 o'clock night, Bill was there. How did you get to go? He says, they tell-- they tell me that I cannot run away in the woods, I cannot be a partisan. You have to bring a package and go tomorrow, because all the Jewish guys are going tomorrow. I says, don't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't listen to me. I made a package for him. The next day, Friday, [? saved ?] themselves 35 young people. So 32 were mobilized by the Russians, and they all went on the-- on the front. Only two-- me, and I told you the other, my friend, he worked for them as secretary. From the-- from the 32, only three survived. One other was wounded, the rest was killed. From our town, was survivors only was about 40 people survived it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russians-- I'm already living under Russia. I need a job. I went to shoe factory, and I says, I can do leather goods. It was, the director from factory was-- his wife was Jewish. So he was kind of close. He says, OK, you work for me. So I worked for him. And he asked me [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] and I-- I'm gonna work for him. I took with me another boy, another man, a Jewish man to work there in that-- that factory, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm working there. We have to clean up the hair. We have to get [? costume ?], cleaned up and work in there. I'm staying there. Of course, they don't give you much-- they give you a little bit of soup in the-- in the afternoon. And I'm very hungry. I'm-- I have no-- no [INAUDIBLE] to get them. One day, I went out on the market to get a piece of bread to buy. The police caught me again. They caught me again. They took me to the police station. I didn't have good papers. They took me I should go to the army again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm telling that I'm working there for-- in the [? sugar ?] factory, leather goods. He says, can I make leather goods for the horses? I says, sure. And he shows me nice, beautiful leather goods. And I says, I can make the same way, but I need the hides, I don't have the hides. He says, he's going to supply with hides. He supplies me with two hides. I make the hides, but I make them fancy. I make the black ones, [INAUDIBLE]. And then I had to pay the Ukrainian guy what he sews the-- for the-- for the horses. So I made two to three weeks, and he sews it. In the meantime, he didn't touch me. I stayed there. And I asked the director he should get me some papers that I can stay. Finally, after a couple of weeks, he give us papers that I can stay there and work in the-- in the-- work for the leather goods. I was staying with the Russians, and the Russians in the shoe factory, work in leather goods.

speaker: interviewee
text: The engineer tells me, we need pieces to go for pumps, pumping out water. It has to be the best made. And I'm thinking to myself, if I make in this-- this-- not going to let go [? the water. ?] You know, the old factory stays. I'm going to get killed for that. But in the meantime, it takes three, four months to make it. So three, four months to live. I don't care. But finally, we made it. We put it in, and it was working good. And they kept us there in the shoe factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what time-- what time of year was this, this whole experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already '43. The end of '43 and beginning of '44. Work and I was free then, if I did for the Russians. Working in the [? sugar ?] factory making leather. And I had papers already. They cannot touch me. But when I finished the job, this takes about six, seven months. I finish the leather goods. I see the end and they have not been given new leather. I went out the director and says to him, can you give me another job? He says, OK, you can go from our city to the big city, Lindberg. You walk, you go there, and you'll be responsible for all the merchandise received.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made me a receiver, but I had to go to the big town to get the-- alcohol, all kind of good stuff. All kind of good stuff for our [? sugar ?] factory. So I work-- I was working there in that capacity till '45 in April.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the director was anti-Semitic. You know, tried to-- tried to take advantage of me. At that time, they were writing documents, whoever belongs to Poland, they could-- Jewish people and Polish people can go to Poland. So I'm registered. I'm going back to Poland. At that time, they wanted-- they call me in to go to army again, but I show them I have papers to go to Poland, so they didn't touch me. And I went to Poland. They give me a machine. At the time, I already knew my girl-- my wife. And it's her mother, and her sister-- they have their belongings. They went-- we didn't have a railway station. We went 30 miles with the machine, and the let us go to the railway station. They threw out all our packages on the ground. I came in and there was a lot of Polish people going to Poland, too. We had to stay waiting for-- for a rail car a month time-- under the blue skies, rain or shine, stay a month time. It was still the war in-- in April. They didn't let us go. The day they finish the war, 8th of May, they give us a-- a rail car. They put our package in, and go to Poland. So we're sitting down. We weren't sure, we thought maybe it can go to Siberia, too. But I saw the direction, and we went to Poland. They took us to-- they all-- to the-- it on there. And they let us-- and the-- they took away a lot of ground from the Germans. We-- we are sitting there, and we could be farmers on German side. So all the Jews together paid up something, and they give us another-- another railroad car. And we want to go back to Lodz. And we all Jewish guys together on that rail truck, went back to Lodz. And I took a-- into Lodz. We need-- we need to stay there somewhere. So at that time, I already had a nice suit. I dressed myself up. I'm going looking for an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I going to the city. One guy says, oh you look so nice and all dressed up. Go on the third floor, there's an empty apartment. And he-- I come in and there are three rooms. I don't even ever think to have three rooms. I'm taking one room, and two rooms, I leave alone. In that one room I came in, me, my-- that time was not my wife yet, Clara, and her sister and her mother. And then, another girl said she wants to see with us. There are four-- there was three, four, five people in the one room. We stayed there in that room for a couple months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we weren't safe there, either. All of a sudden, we had the [INAUDIBLE] Polish partisans nationalists, they're killing Jews. We had to run away from them. We find that we had to run away to the American zone. In order to go to the American zone, you have to go to Berlin and from farther out. One guy-- Jewish guy in-- in Szczecin it's right on the border, he had connections to put you on the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went into Szczecin, this from Lodz to Szczecin, it may be 1,000 miles. [INAUDIBLE]. I come in, and I says, I want to go to Berlin. He says, oh, it's a big line. You have to-- you're gonna be in two weeks. You have to stay two weeks. It's going to cost you 4,000 marks for each person. Four persons-- 16,000, and you have to wait two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I'm saying and talking to him, a Russian guy comes in, and he looks like a driver. He takes some whiskey, buying-- he has a-- he had a little store. He takes whiskey. He takes meat, and he doesn't pay anything and he runs out. I said that is the driver that takes it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm following the guy. I'm following him, and I talk to him Russia. I can talk Russian. I says, can you take us? He says, let's go to the lieutenant. Let him talk. We go back to lieutenant, and I says-- he says, yeah, let's take on the four. If that guy gives us 20 guys, it's too dangerous. Four guys we can take. And he tells us, come at 9 o'clock in the evening, we'll take you to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come at 9 o'clock in the evening, three wives-- my wife, her sister-- they call me the three wives-- and her mother. We went there. Went in the machine. We have to pay the Polish border, the Russian border and go to Berlin. I says, wait a minute. We have to be careful. So-- so we had some blankets. We put the blankets on top of the heads at night, cover it up, and then they should look like a head. We keep the hands high, and when we pass the-- the border, they wouldn't know who it is. That's what we did. We came to the Polish-- Polish border, they let us go on. The Russian border, we were careful. We had to pick up our hands like that. We kept quiet inside underneath the blanket. He let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came into Berlin, 12 o'clock at night. I see over there Stalin here and all kind of pictures there. He says, go down. I says, where am I going down? I have to get a place where? He didn't want to listen. Go down and that's all. Pay me. I don't know about how much to pay him. I told him 4,000 per person. He thought 4,000 for all four. So when were riding, I says to my wife, keep 12,000 separate and 4,000. If he says 4,000 for everybody, it's fine. If he says 16,000, we give him the rest. I give him the packet of 4,000, he says, OK. Went down. We were in Berlin, 12 o'clock at night. I went down by myself first. I look and see some-- some ditch here. I says, OK, come down. We went in the ditch. The package was there in the ditch, the same ditch, we didn't realize. It was dark, anyway. We stay in the ditch all night, in the morning, we see what's gone up. All of a sudden, in the morning, someone from the ditch coming up. What is it? From the ditch coming up, this was a broken elevator. They were driving-- riding the elevator underneath. So I went down there, and I see a lot of people. I ask another German with the-- with the [? hand ?] wagon and you paid him. And he was going to take us to the Jewish people there on the French zone. So I have to go with him, but the mother took a [INAUDIBLE], and she went first. It took me maybe half a day, 12 o'clock in the afternoon till I came in with the packages in the French zone. I came in the French zone. He says to me, I already loaded, we have 200 people there. We cannot have you there. You have to go in the Russian zone and rendezvous.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I don't want to go there to the Russia. I want to stay here. No, [INAUDIBLE]. Put the package on the machine again. We went back. Our rendezvous [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish [? commander ?] came in there. He came in there, maybe a thousand people there. In each hall, three-- three stories high with beds. So we had a cot. He gave us a bed, you know, some food, one on top of the other one. And we stayed there maybe four weeks. All of a sudden, one time in the evening, came-- there came a news that all-- the Russians are going to come at night, they're going to take us, and they're gonna put is in Siberia. This was maybe 8 o'clock. Until 9 o'clock, the rail-- the train was going into [INAUDIBLE]. We all run away. We run away in a [? street ?] [? car ?] and go out to the French zone. In the French zone, we stay there in the French zone a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.
